title_en: Bada'i as-Sana'i fi Tartib ash-Shara'i: Organization and Vision
title_ar: بدائع الصنائع في ترتيب الشرائع: التنظيم والرؤية
content_en:
Bada'i as-Sana'i fi Tartib ash-Shara'i (The Masterworks of Craftsmanship in Arranging the Divine Laws) by Abu Bakr ibn Mas'ud al-Kasani (d. 587 AH / 1191 CE) announces its organizing principle in its full title: the arrangement (tartib) of the divine laws (shara'i) according to a systematic framework. This emphasis on arrangement distinguishes Al-Kasani's work from the organic organization of earlier Hanafi manuals and gives the work its distinctive pedagogical character.

The 'arrangement' that al-Kasani provides is not merely a reordering of topics but a systematic framework for understanding each legal institution. For every major area of law, he identifies: (1) the conditions required for the legal act to be valid; (2) the pillars (arkan) that constitute the act's essential core; (3) the factors that invalidate the act; and (4) the subsidiary questions (furu') that follow from the basic framework. This explicit analytical structure makes Bada'i as-Sana'i unusually transparent in its organization and unusually useful as an educational text.

Al-Kasani was a student of 'Ala ad-Din as-Samarqandi, the author of Tuhfat al-Fuqaha — a well-regarded Hanafi legal text that itself had a clearer organizational scheme than earlier manuals. Bada'i as-Sana'i is in part a commentary on Tuhfat al-Fuqaha that expands as-Samarqandi's positions with fuller analysis. The teacher-student relationship and the work's commentary structure are embedded in the title's reference to 'arrangement' — al-Kasani is organizing and systematizing the tradition he received from his teacher.

The Hanafi school in Central Asia, where al-Kasani was educated, was characterized by rigorous scholarly organization. The great madrasas of Samarkand and Bukhara produced scholars who thought carefully about how to transmit and organize the school's vast accumulated learning. Al-Kasani's systematic approach reflects this tradition — an approach that values clarity of organization as a scholarly virtue alongside comprehensiveness of coverage.

Bada'i as-Sana'i covers the complete range of Hanafi law: taharah, salah, zakat, sawm, hajj, commercial transactions, family law, criminal law, and judicial procedure. In each area, al-Kasani's systematic framework — conditions, pillars, invalidating factors, subsidiary questions — provides students with a reliable organizational map that makes the material learnable and navigable.

The work's influence extended beyond the Hanafi school: its systematic organizational framework was noted and referenced by scholars of other schools as a model of clear legal organization. In the modern period, its explicit identification of conditions and pillars has made Bada'i as-Sana'i particularly useful for comparative legal analysis — its clear articulation of what the Hanafi school considers essential to each legal institution makes comparison with other schools' frameworks straightforward.
